description: marmot-nids signature rule set v1.0 — covers volumetric DoS, reconnaissance scans, brute-force attacks, and protocol anomalies.

signatures.name: SYN Flood
signatures.description: High-volume TCP SYN packets with no corresponding ACK responses. Indicates a TCP SYN flood DoS attack exhausting connection tables on the target host.

signatures.name: RST Flood
signatures.description: High-volume TCP RST packets injected to forcibly terminate established connections, causing denial-of-service without requiring a volume-based IP signature.

signatures.name: ICMP Flood
signatures.description: High-rate ICMP echo requests indicative of a ping flood denial-of-service attack. Saturates the target network link or exhausts CPU handling echo replies.

signatures.name: TCP NULL Scan
signatures.description: TCP packets with no control flags set (flags=0x00). Used during network reconnaissance to probe open ports and infer firewall state — RFC 793-compliant hosts respond with RST to closed ports and silently drop packets on open ports.

signatures.name: TCP FIN Scan
signatures.description: TCP packets carrying only the FIN flag, sent without a prior SYN handshake. Used for stealthy port scanning that bypasses some stateless packet filters and older firewall configurations.

signatures.name: TCP XMAS Scan
signatures.description: TCP packets with FIN, PSH, and URG flags simultaneously set (the Christmas-tree pattern). Used for stealthy reconnaissance — RFC 793-compliant hosts send RST to closed ports and ignore packets on open ports.

signatures.name: SSH Brute Force
signatures.description: High packet count on SSH port (22) within a short time window, indicative of automated credential guessing or dictionary-based brute-force against the SSH service.

signatures.name: HTTP Brute Force
signatures.description: High packet count on HTTP port (80) within a short time window, indicative of web application credential stuffing or form-based brute-force login attempts.

signatures.name: HTTPS Brute Force
signatures.description: High packet count on HTTPS port (443) within a short time window, indicative of web application credential stuffing over encrypted transport.

signatures.name: DNS Amplification Attack
signatures.description: High-volume large UDP responses from DNS port (53) with disproportionate payload size, indicative of a DNS reflection amplification DDoS attack using open resolvers.

signatures.name: RDP Brute Force
signatures.description: High packet count on RDP port (3389) within a short time window, indicative of automated Windows Remote Desktop credential guessing or password-spraying.

signatures.name: SMB Service Scan
signatures.description: Short-lived flow to SMB port (445) with minimal packet exchange, indicative of automated reconnaissance targeting Windows file sharing services and lateral movement paths.

signatures.name: Telnet Access Detected
signatures.description: Active Telnet session detected on port 23. Telnet transmits credentials and session data in cleartext and must not be used on any production or internal network segment.

signatures.name: Large ICMP Payload
signatures.description: ICMP packets with abnormally large payload (mean >= 1000 bytes), potentially indicating a Ping-of-Death variant, ICMP tunneling for covert C2, or data exfiltration via the ICMP protocol.

signatures.name: FTP Brute Force
signatures.description: High packet count on FTP port (21) within a short time window, indicative of automated FTP credential guessing or dictionary attack against cleartext file transfer services.

signatures.name: UDP High-Volume Data Exfiltration
signatures.description: High-volume UDP flow with large mean payload size in a short period, potentially indicating data exfiltration over an unencrypted non-standard UDP channel bypassing DLP controls.

signatures.name: Low-and-Slow Port Scan
signatures.description: Very few TCP SYN packets sent at low rate, consistent with a deliberate slow scan designed to evade rate-based detection thresholds and IDS alert suppression windows.
